ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Chicken Caesar Salad; mapped to the nearest parent class 'salad food product' (FOODON_00002809). A more specific code does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers composite salad dishes; no dedicated Chicken Caesar Salad code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Chicken Caesar Salad as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Chicken Caesar Salad is approximately 300 g, comprising romaine lettuce, grilled chicken, Caesar dressing, Parmesan shavings, and croutons.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Caesar Salad — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Chicken Caesar Salad (cr_id G00010648). All per-100g values are preserved from the source record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 35188, Caesar salad with chicken) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed salad with protein and fat-dominant dressing
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Caesar Salad. GI estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed green salad with protein and high-fat dressing, which has negligible carbohydrate content (3.3g/100g). Glycemic load per 300g serving = (15 × 3.3 × 3) / 100 = 1.5 / 2 ≈ 0.7 (rounded). The very low carbohydrate content renders the GI clinically insignificant.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1210 mg/100g, far exceeding the 600 mg/100g threshold; potassium is only 29 mg/100g, providing negligible offsetting effect.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Chicken Caesar Salad due to its sodium content of 1210 mg/100g; if consumed, request dressing on the side and omit Parmesan and croutons to reduce sodium load substantially.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content at 3.3 g/100g with sugar at 2.81 g/100g; high fat content further slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Caesar Salad poses minimal glycaemic risk for patients with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content; however, patients should request croutons be omitted and verify that the Caesar dressing contains no added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is very high at 57.8 g/100g with estimated saturated fat of approximately 9.5 g/100g, and sodium at 1210 mg/100g significantly elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Chicken Caesar Salad in its standard form; if consumed, request dressing on the side, omit croutons and Parmesan, and choose a grilled chicken preparation to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; sodium at 1210 mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request an olive oil and lemon dressing substitution and omit Parmesan and croutons to reduce saturated fat and sodium-driven inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Caesar Salad is a Western salad dish of American origin, traditionally attributed to restaurateur Caesar Cardini in Tijuana, Mexico, in the 1920s. It consists of crisp romaine lettuce leaves topped with grilled or pan-seared chicken breast, shaved or grated Parmesan cheese, crunchy croutons, and a rich Caesar dressing made from anchovies, egg yolk, garlic, lemon juice, Worcestershire sauce, and olive oil. The dish delivers a bold umami flavour profile with creamy, tangy, and savoury notes, contrasted by the crunch of croutons and the freshness of romaine. As served in Singapore food service contexts, the dressing is typically pre-mixed and applied generously, resulting in a high fat content of 57.8 g/100g and a very high sodium content of 1210 mg/100g. Protein content is relatively low at 2.17 g/100g for the composite dish, reflecting the dressing-dominant composition of the HPB-recorded entry. Carbohydrates are minimal at 3.3 g/100g, making the dish low-glycaemic. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its extreme sodium and saturated fat load. It is acceptable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes in small portions without croutons. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal-certified in standard preparations due to anchovies and non-halal Worcestershire sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Caesar salad with chicken?
Is Caesar salad good for diabetics?
Can I eat Caesar salad if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of Caesar salad?
Is Caesar salad high in fat?
What allergens are in Caesar salad?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010648, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from the closest USDA FoodData Central equivalent and is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for Caesar salad with chicken (NDB 35188) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this record. This is an adapted value and is flagged accordingly. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this Chicken Caesar Salad entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish as of 2026. 3. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'Indian'; this has been corrected to 'Western' as Chicken Caesar Salad is a Western dish of American/Mexican-American origin. 4. Protein at 2.17 g/100g is low relative to a typical chicken salad; this reflects the HPB source record for the composite dish as a whole (dressing-dominant composition) and is preserved as reported. No zero-macro justification is required as no core macro is zero. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 15, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for mixed salads with protein and fat-dominant dressings; the very low carbohydrate content (3.3 g/100g) renders the GI clinically insignificant. Glycemic load per 300g serving ≈ 0.7. 6. Halal status is uncertain in standard preparations due to anchovies and Worcestershire sauce; is_halal is set to false as a conservative default.
